title: NATO Summit Eve: Ankara Locks Down, Crackdown Widens, and Turkey Takes Center Stage
subhead: As leaders converge on Ankara for the first NATO summit on Turkish soil, Turkish authorities have detained over 100 protesters and dozens of journalists and activists in a sweeping pre-summit security operation — while Erdogan prepares to host Trump and the alliance at a moment of deep transatlantic strain.

sections.heading: Pre-Summit Crackdown: Over 100 Detained as Ankara Shuts Down
sections.body:
Turkish authorities detained more than 100 members of the Communist Party of Turkey (TKP) during anti-NATO protests in Ankara and Istanbul on Sunday, according to the party's own statement. Protesters marched to Ankara's central Kizilay Square chanting 'Murderer NATO, get out of our country' and 'No passage to NATO,' before riot police dispersed crowds with tear gas. Separate protests in Istanbul's Taksim Square and Kadikoy district drew hundreds more, Reuters reports.

In a parallel crackdown, police detained 39 suspects across eight provinces targeting the youth wing of the outlawed leftist group THKP/C-DEV YOL, and another 28 suspects in Kocaeli province allegedly linked to Islamic State and leftist militant groups, according to state agency Anadolu. Most notably, raids across multiple provinces targeted journalists and civil society: editors from the T24 and Oda TV news portals were detained, alongside academics and trade union members, Halk TV and Cumhuriyet reported.

The Turkish Journalists' Association (TGC) and the Progressive Lawyers' Association (CHD) demanded the release of those held, calling the raids an attempt to intimidate opposition voices. DEM Party co-chair Tuncer Bakirhan called the detentions 'unacceptable,' declaring: 'The country has been fully turned into a detention centre by using the NATO summit as an excuse. We are living through days of undeclared martial law.' CHP's court-appointed chairman Kemal Kilicdaroglu also criticized the operations. Authorities last month arrested 103 people in anti-terror raids in Ankara, with 225 detained overall.
sections.whyItMatters: The scale of the pre-summit security sweep — combining legitimate counterterrorism operations with what critics call suppression of dissent — sets the tone for how Erdogan's Turkey presents itself to the alliance: orderly, controlled, and intolerant of public opposition to NATO.

sections.heading: NATO Ankara Summit: Trump Demands 'Loyalty,' Not Just Money
sections.body:
The NATO summit convenes in Ankara on Tuesday and Wednesday, the first time the alliance has met in Turkey. But the agenda is dominated by a single variable: what Donald Trump wants. The U.S. president arrives Monday evening for a bilateral meeting with Erdogan before the formal summit, where he will hold separate talks with Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy and Syrian President Ahmad al-Sharaa on Wednesday.

At last month's White House meeting, NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte presented Trump with a chart labeled 'The Trump Trillion' showing $1.2 trillion in allied spending since 2017 and a $300 billion backlog in European orders for American military equipment. Trump's response, per the CSMonitor: 'We don't need their money — we don't need anything. I just want loyalty.' He suggested he might have skipped the summit entirely were it not being hosted by Erdogan — a sign that even the personal rapport between the two leaders may not prevent turbulence.

U.S. Ambassador to NATO Matt Whitaker said allies have committed nearly $139 billion in additional defense spending since the Hague summit, but stressed that Trump 'expects all allies to step up immediately' to the 5% of GDP target. Whitaker said the U.S. goal remains 'shifting the burden of the conventional defense of Europe to our European allies and Canada.' The Pentagon has already announced it will scale back the troops, warships, aircraft, and drones it would provide if a NATO ally comes under attack — and will withdraw 5,000 U.S. troops from Europe.

The summit declaration, kept deliberately short to avoid confrontation with Trump, will call for a 'stronger Europe in a stronger NATO' and state that Iran should never have a nuclear weapon while calling on Tehran to 'respect freedom of navigation' in the Strait of Hormuz, according to diplomats cited by Kyiv Post.
sections.whyItMatters: This is a pivotal summit for Turkey's role in NATO: Erdogan's personal rapport with Trump may be the glue holding the alliance together, but the summit's minimalist declaration and Trump's 'loyalty' demand expose the fragility of the transatlantic relationship at a moment when Europe's security architecture is being restructured.

sections.heading: Turkey's Paradox: Indispensable yet Distrusted Inside NATO
sections.body:
For host nation Turkey, the summit presents a contradiction. Yasar Aydin, researcher at the Center for Applied Turkey Studies (SWP), argues that Turkey is 'becoming ever more important for Europe's security, yet political trust in the Turkish leadership within the alliance continues to decline.' Soner Cagaptay of the Washington Institute describes Turkey as a 'swing state' within NATO — 'essential yet unpredictable, indispensable yet difficult to manage.'

Turkey's ambivalent posture toward Russia, its fierce rhetoric against Israel, and long-standing tensions with Greece continue to raise questions about its reliability. Foreign Minister Hakan Fidan recently delivered what Israeli officials described as 'incitement to genocide' in an interview on CNN Turk, saying Israel was 'not only Turkey's problem' and calling it 'a burden.' These tensions complicate NATO's southern flank agenda.

At the same time, Turkey's control of the Bosphorus and Dardanelles straits remains a strategic asset without substitute, particularly as Russia's Black Sea Fleet depends on these waterways for Mediterranean access. Turkey's defense industry — particularly Baykar's drone exports — and its role as a mediator between Russia and Ukraine give it leverage that no ally can simply wish away. Fidan visited Moscow in June to reiterate Turkey's offer to host Russia-Ukraine peace talks and discuss Black Sea shipping safety, meeting both Lavrov and Putin, according to Reuters.

A Xinhua analysis notes that while European allies see Russia as the defining threat, 'Turkey and other southern flank members' consider the Middle East and Mediterranean 'no less pressing.' As Aydin put it: 'A common strategy is difficult to formulate when its members do not share the same view of where the greatest threat lies.'
sections.whyItMatters: Turkey's strategic value is rising in parallel with the distrust it generates among allies — and the Ankara summit forces NATO to confront both dimensions simultaneously.

sections.heading: Trump-Zelenskyy and Trump-al-Sharaa Meetings on the Sidelines
sections.body:
Trump's bilateral meetings on Wednesday carry significant implications for Turkey's neighborhood. His session with Zelenskyy comes as both leaders spoke separately with Putin on Saturday. Zelenskyy said there is 'a real prospect of ending this war,' and a senior U.S. official said Trump feels 'a real sense of urgency' to end the conflict, adding that Trump is expected to follow up with Putin after the meeting. Ukraine's ambassador to Ankara has told Reuters that Kyiv wants deeper defense industry cooperation with Turkey, including joint production.

The meeting with Syria's Ahmad al-Sharaa is the most unexpected. Trump has repeatedly suggested Syria should deal with Hezbollah instead of Israel doing so — a proposal al-Sharaa has publicly declined, saying he has no interest in opening that front. U.S. officials provided no details about the goals of the meeting. The al-Sharaa meeting is significant for Turkey, which has been a key backer of the forces that ousted Assad and maintains troops in northern Syria.

European allies and Canada are set to commit €70 billion ($80 billion) annually for 2026 and 2027 in military aid to Ukraine, including €30 billion per year from an EU loan, diplomats told Kyiv Post. The pledge is largely symbolic — aimed at showing Trump that allies have taken over Ukraine financing — but Zelenskyy's attendance at the Tuesday night leaders' dinner (not the formal summit the next day) signals the careful calibration around his presence.
sections.whyItMatters: Both sideline meetings — one expected, one surprising — shape Turkey's strategic environment: Ukraine talks affect Turkey's Black Sea security posture, while the al-Sharaa meeting could reshape how regional actors approach the post-Assad Levant.

sections.heading: Strait of Hormuz Crisis Continues to Pressure Turkey's Energy Bill
sections.body:
The Strait of Hormuz remains a live crisis as the NATO summit opens. Iran's ambassador to China announced new service fees for ships transiting Hormuz, despite U.S. objections, according to MSN. Bloomberg reports that oil and gas tankers are beginning to use an Oman-side coastal route to transit the strait, though operations remain fragile after recent U-turns. The 2026 oil price surge — driven by the Hormuz closure and the broader Iran conflict — has reignited inflation globally, and Turkey, as a major energy importer, is particularly exposed.

The NATO summit declaration will call on Iran to 'respect freedom of navigation' in the strait but will not mention any potential European or NATO mission to keep it open, as the situation remains volatile despite a fragile ceasefire. Several European countries have moved naval assets near the strait. Turkey, which maintains diplomatic channels to both Tehran and Washington, has positioned itself as a mediator in the broader Iran conflict, with Erdogan stating Turkey 'chose diplomacy over escalation.'

However, Turkey's energy import bill has been significantly inflated by the Hormuz disruption. The country sourced roughly 80% of its oil imports from the Middle East prior to the conflict, making alternative supply routes and the Oman corridor operationally critical.
sections.whyItMatters: The Hormuz crisis directly hits Turkey's energy costs and inflation trajectory. Turkey's diplomatic positioning as a mediator gives it geopolitical leverage, but the economic damage from disrupted energy transit is immediate and measurable.

sections.heading: Russian Ballistic Missiles Hit Kyiv on Summit Eve
sections.body:
Russian ballistic missiles struck Kyiv early Monday morning, Ukraine's air force confirmed, on the eve of the NATO summit in Ankara. The timing was unmistakable — a signal from Moscow that the war in Ukraine remains active and that Russia can escalate at will, regardless of NATO's diplomatic calendar.

The strike underscores the urgency of the Ukraine agenda at the summit. While Zelenskyy will attend a leaders' dinner Tuesday, he is excluded from the formal Wednesday session — a deliberate choice by NATO to avoid antagonizing Trump, whose patience with Ukraine funding has worn thin. The pledge of €70 billion annually from European allies and Canada is designed in part to demonstrate that Europe can fund Ukraine without U.S. assistance, though whether the money translates into timely military support remains an open question.

Turkey's role as a potential mediator remains in play: Fidan's June visit to Moscow included a proposal for a limited ceasefire on ports and energy infrastructure in the Black Sea, and Turkey has previously hosted unsuccessful talks between Russian and Ukrainian delegations in 2022.
sections.whyItMatters: The Kyiv strike is a reminder that the NATO summit is taking place while an active war rages on the alliance's doorstep. Turkey's mediation offer remains on the table, but Russia's escalation signals it sees no urgency to accept.

bottomLine: Turkey hosts the first NATO summit on its soil amid a sweeping domestic crackdown, deep transatlantic fractures, and a Russian missile barrage on Kyiv timed for maximum impact. The summit's minimalist declaration — kept short to avoid provoking Trump — reflects the reality that the alliance's biggest challenges are not about strategy but about managing a U.S. president who now demands 'loyalty' rather than burden-sharing. For Erdogan, the summit is a showcase of Turkey's strategic indispensability: his personal rapport with Trump keeps the American president at the table, Turkey controls the straits Russia needs, and its defense industry and mediation channels give it real leverage. But the pre-summit detentions of journalists, activists, and protesters — and the opposition's description of 'undeclared martial law' — reveal the domestic cost of that positioning. The Trump-al-Sharaa sideline meeting is an unexpected wildcard that could reshape Turkey's Syria calculus. Meanwhile, the Hormuz crisis continues to inflate Turkey's energy bill, and Russian missiles over Kyiv remind everyone that the alliance's core deterrence mission is far from resolved.
sources: Reuters, AP News, CSMonitor, Xinhua, Yahoo News, CNBC, National Post, Kyiv Post, Bloomberg, Manila Times, Anadolu Agency (via Yahoo), Al-Monitor, Daily Sabah, SWP/DGAP (via Xinhua), Washington Institute (via Xinhua), Seeking Alpha, MSN
